ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for curry udon with tempura prawn. Mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for curry udon with fried ebi. A00LC (noodle-based dishes) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional noodle preparations of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of curry udon with fried ebi tempura is approximately 450 g, comprising udon noodles in curry broth with vegetables and 3 pieces of ebi tempura.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Udon with Fried Ebi — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for curry udon with fried ebi (F05060041). All per-100g values preserved from the HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (udon noodle dish with fried prawn) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for curry udon with fried ebi tempura in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous food: udon noodles (GI ~55, University of Sydney GI Database). The curry sauce, potato, and tempura batter components add refined carbohydrates but the overall dish GI is anchored to the udon noodle GI value. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (61.2 g from 450 g × 13.61 g/100g) ÷ 100 = 33.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 293 mg/100 g; a full 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1,319 mg sodium, representing over half the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, avoid drinking the full curry broth, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake from a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load per serving (~33.7) driven by refined udon noodles (GI ~55), tempura batter, potato, and curry roux starch; only 1.21 g/100 g dietary fibre to blunt glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, request smaller noodle servings with extra non-starchy vegetables, substitute plain grilled prawn for tempura, and choose a curry powder-based broth over starch-thickened roux to reduce glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried ebi tempura contributes saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g) and dietary AGEs; high sodium load (293 mg/100 g) adds cardiovascular risk; low potassium and fibre content provide limited cardioprotective offset.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request grilled or steamed prawn instead of tempura, avoid consuming the full broth to limit sodium, and keep portion size to half a standard bowl to manage saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; refined udon noodles and low fibre content provide no meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute grilled or steamed prawn for ebi tempura, request reduced-sodium broth, and consider replacing udon with a higher-fibre noodle to reduce pro-inflammatory dietary load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry udon with fried ebi tempura is a Japanese-influenced noodle dish popular in Singapore's food court and casual dining settings. It consists of thick, chewy wheat-based udon noodles served in a rich Japanese curry sauce — typically a starch-thickened roux flavoured with curry spices, often containing diced carrot and potato — topped with three pieces of ebi (prawn) tempura, which are whole prawns coated in a light wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The dish delivers a contrast of textures: soft, slippery noodles in a thick, mildly spiced curry broth alongside crunchy, hot tempura prawns. Per 100 g, it provides 111 kcal, 3.22 g protein, 4.86 g fat, 13.61 g carbohydrate, 1.21 g dietary fibre, and 293 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), but the glycaemic load per standard 450 g bowl is high (~33.7), making portion control critical for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is moderate-to-high concern for hypertension due to sodium from the curry broth and soy-based seasoning, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from deep-frying and the high sodium load. It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis without modification, as deep-frying generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation. Allergens include gluten (wheat udon and tempura batter), crustaceans (prawn), eggs (tempura batter), and soy (broth seasoning).

Is curry udon suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05060041) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous composite. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (udon noodles, GI ~55). All values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous composite data for udon noodle dish with fried prawn, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F05060041, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA to support these values without unacceptable estimation error. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on udon noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in an approved source. 4. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as GI (55) × available carbohydrate per 450 g serving (61.2 g) ÷ 100 = 33.7. 5. The dish is classified as Japanese-Singaporean fusion; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Japanese per the mandatory language rules for Singaporean dishes with Japanese cuisine influence. 6. Halal status is marked false as Japanese curry roux products may contain non-halal ingredients (e.g., pork-derived additives); consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
